paragraphs.text: Looking over the DFA's website, this seems like a great initative that I support. I still remember when the dark patterns hall of shame was first published. When for the first time you required credit card information for a free trial that would automatically charge you if you didn't manually cancel your trial. How frightfully precise researchers could target people with targeted ads. The fact you have to jump through a whole circus of hoops to delete your Amazon account. How every website prominently displays an accept all cookies banner with, at best, a dark and dreary (i.e. a 'bad' looking) button to reject cookies, and usually a my settings button where, again at best, there's a no to all button, but often still you have to individually disable every specific party that wants to place cookies on your computer (especially as according to the rules and my moral compass I can totally make a website that does use cookies and doesn't need to ask permission because it's about basic and minimal functionality not about data scraping and selling people to faceless corperations). When the USA FTC forced people to clearly label their paid videos as ads or paid by and was for a change ahead of the EU and we still don't have that requirement as clear as that. How easily companies get to manipulate the truth (I think most people would call that lying) about their digital goods and serices, claiming rarity or limited availability. How stores tell you there are only a few items left, or hundreds of people are viewing an item. How prices start at, but that's not what they put in your shopping basket. Unavoidable charges not inlcuded in prices. Absolutely unenforcable (and in my opinion illegal EULAs). Having to clear your browser history and a few other steps before buying plane tickets to avoid higher prices (or even worse, dynamic pricing). Straight up real gambling in videogames for children (but fake gambling like Balatro being attacked). In fact, you might want to go a little further and also look at the practice of selling videogames to consumers, building in a central server dependancy, and then later shutting down those servers without removing that dependancy making the video games literally unplayable. We used to have big multiplayer games with community servers and p2p, but now it's all developer or publisher controlled. Even for single player games. Meanwhile communities are running their own Massive Multiplayer Online game servers, so it is possible.
